literal: the going-out into Brahman; *nirvāṇa* (nir- + √vā 'to blow', past participle, 'blown out, as a flame') compounded with *brahman* — read as a locative or genitive tatpuruṣa, 'the going-out [of ego and desire] that is in / unto Brahman'
invariant_definition: *Brahma-nirvāṇa* is a compound for liberation characteristic of the *Bhagavad-Gītā*: the going-out (*nirvāṇa*) of the fire of ego and desire taken, in the same movement, as a reaching of or mergence into Brahman. The image in *nirvāṇa* is a flame going out (nir + √vā, 'to blow out'): when the fuel of attachment is withdrawn, the flame of craving goes out. The *Gītā* joins this image to *brahman*, so that what goes out is the limited, desire-driven self, while what is reached is the unconditioned reality of Brahman. The compound is largely confined to the *Gītā* among the major texts: it occurs there a small number of times (2.72; 5.24–26; cf. 6.15, *brahma-bhūta*) and is uncommon elsewhere. *Nirvāṇa* is also the central term for the Buddhist goal, where the going-out of the three fires (*rāga, dveṣa, moha*) is described without positing a permanent self or a substrate into which one merges. In compounding *nirvāṇa* with *brahman*, the *Gītā* uses the going-out image while naming Brahman as the absolute reached.

school_framing.shared_core: *Brahma-nirvāṇa* names the going-out of the desire-and-ego fire taken at once as a reaching of, or mergence in, Brahman — liberation described through a flame-going-out image with Brahman as the absolute reached (the *Gītā*'s compound). Vedāntic schools share this Brahman-positive reading; they differ on what 'reaching Brahman' consists in.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Buddhist *nibbāna/nirvāṇa* describes the going-out of the three fires, the unconditioned, without positing an *ātman* or *brahman* substrate — an apophatic terminus. The *Gītā*'s compound names Brahman as the absolute reached. Within Vedānta: Advaita reads *brahma-nirvāṇa* as identity-realization and the jīva's mergence; Viśiṣṭādvaita reads it as the reaching of the Brahman-state under Bhagavān, the distinct self persisting rather than being erased.

per_school.definition: For Advaita *brahma-nirvāṇa* is the extinction of the individuality founded on *avidyā* and the realization of identity with *brahman*: the 'blowing-out' is the dissolution of the false, desire-structured self, and what is 'attained' is what one always was, the non-dual reality. Śaṅkara reads the *Gītā*'s usages as describing the *brahma-bhūta* state — the knower become Brahman — for whom the metaphor of nirvāṇa marks the cessation of saṃsāric becoming, not annihilation into a void. The jīva's apparent mergence is the removal of the limiting adjuncts that never truly divided it from Brahman.

Rāmānuja resists any reading of *brahma-nirvāṇa* as the erasure or annihilation of the self. The compound names the soul's reaching the Brahman-state — abiding in the condition of *brahman* — but for him this is the liberated self's attainment of its own untrammelled nature in relation to and under Bhagavān, not absorption that cancels the distinct *jīva*. The 'extinction' is of bondage, sin, and the disturbances of craving (the *Gītā* itself lists the destruction of sins and doubts), while the self persists in its realized, Lord-oriented condition.
per_school.register_tag: M+S; prapanna (reaching the Brahman-state, not absorption-erasure)

per_school.definition: In Buddhist usage *nibbāna/nirvāṇa* is the going-out of the three fires — *rāga* (greed), *dveṣa* (hatred), *moha* (delusion) — and the unconditioned (*asaṃskṛta*) state freed of craving and rebirth. The classical traditions do not posit a permanent self (*anātman*) or a Brahman-substrate as that which is liberated or into which one merges; the terminus is described apophatically, by negation rather than as union with a positive absolute, and Madhyamaka does not assign nirvāṇa the categories 'existent' or 'non-existent'. The *Gītā*'s compound *brahma-nirvāṇa* uses the same going-out image but names Brahman as the absolute reached.
primary_loci: Dhammapada / the Ādittapariyāya (Fire) discourse (the going-out of the fires)
Mūla-Madhyamaka-Kārikā 25 (nirvāṇa as the unconditioned, neither existent nor non-existent)

textual_history.summary: The *Gītā*'s compound *brahma-nirvāṇa* turns on the image carried by *nirvāṇa* itself — a flame blown out (nir-√vā) once its fuel is withdrawn. The Vedic and Upaniṣadic texts have their own long career of the flame and the hidden light: in the Ṛgveda Agni is the fire seated in the secret place (*guhā*) and kindled in the heart; in the Upaniṣads the inner self is figured as a flame, 'like a light without smoke' (Kaṭha 2.1.13), the thumb-sized *puruṣa* dwelling in the heart, and the self-luminous Brahman 'by whose shining all this shines'. The *Gītā* joins the going-out image to *brahman*, so that what is blown out is the desire-and-ego fire while what is reached is Brahman. The *Gītā* does not cite the Vedic heart-flame when it says *brahma-nirvāṇa*; the connection to the older fire-and-light imagery is a shared vocabulary rather than a cited source. The stages below give the Vedic and Upaniṣadic loci of that imagery alongside the *Gītā*'s compound; see also agni, jyoti, guha, hrdaya, and dipa.

stages.sanskrit: haste dadhāno nṛmṇā viśvāny ame devān dhād guhā niṣīdan | … | vidantīm atra naro dhiyaṃdhā hṛdā yat taṣṭān mantrām̐ aśaṃsan
stages.rendering: Holding in his hand all manly powers, he set the gods [in safety] as he sat down in the secret place (guhā). … There the men, holding the thought, found him — when, fashioned by the heart, they spoke forth the mantras.
stages.note: Agni, the fire, is the god who 'sits down in the guhā' — the hidden place, read by the tradition as the cave or secret of the heart — and is found there by seers whose mantras are 'fashioned by the heart' (hṛdā taṣṭān). This is the earliest pole of the imagery: a flame at once concealed and luminous, lodged in a secret inner place. (Caution: the RV verse-divisions in the Aufrecht/Saṃhitā edition do not map one-to-one onto the Padapāṭha pāda-numbering; the loci are cited as printed in our saved source.)

stages.sanskrit: guhā carantaṃ sakhibhiḥ śivebhir divo yahvībhir na guhā babhūva
stages.rendering: [They found] him moving in the secret place (guhā) with his benign companions; by the mighty [streams] of heaven the secret place was [his] no longer [i.e. he was disclosed].
stages.note: The recurring Ṛgvedic formula of the fire hidden in, and recovered from, a secret inner place: in the first half Agni moves 'in the guhā', and the second half marks his disclosure. A modern psychological reading of the *guhā* as a hidden register of the psyche exists (Sri Aurobindo, The Secret of the Veda); it is one interpretive lens and is not the basis for the plain sense given here.

stages.sanskrit: aṅguṣṭhamātraḥ puruṣo jyotir iva adhūmakaḥ | īśāno bhūtabhavyasya sa evādya sa u śvaḥ
stages.rendering: The Person the measure of a thumb, like a light without smoke — lord of what has been and what will be — he is today and he is tomorrow.
stages.note: The Vedic hidden fire is here interiorised as the *puruṣa* in the heart, figured through a flame-simile: the thumb-sized Person is 'like a light without smoke' (jyotir iva adhūmakaḥ) — the smokelessness qualifies the light-simile, not the Person directly. Here that light figures the deathless self. The thumb-measured Person seated in the heart recurs at Kaṭha 2.3.17 (kau_6.17): 'aṅguṣṭhamātraḥ puruṣo 'ntarātmā sadā janānāṃ hṛdaye saṃniviṣṭaḥ' — 'the thumb-sized Person, the inner self, ever seated in the heart of beings.'

stages.locus: Kaṭha 2.2.15 (= GRETIL kau_5.15); parallel at Muṇḍaka 2.2.10 and Śvetāśvatara 6.14
stages.sanskrit: na tatra sūryo bhāti na candratārakaṃ nemā vidyuto bhānti kuto 'yam agniḥ | tam eva bhāntam anu bhāti sarvaṃ tasya bhāsā sarvam idaṃ vibhāti
stages.rendering: There the sun does not shine, nor moon and stars; these lightnings do not shine — how then this fire? Him alone, as he shines, all things shine after; by his shining all this shines.
stages.note: The inner light is self-luminous, the source from which even fire (agni) borrows its shining. Commenting on the parallel Muṇḍaka verse, Śaṅkara glosses the self-luminosity with the lamp-image — 'no lamp shines after another lamp' (nahi pradīpaḥ pradīpāntaram anubhāti, Brahma-sūtra-bhāṣya) — keeping the flame-figure in view. This is the light reached in *brahma-nirvāṇa*: not blown out but unborrowed.

stages.locus: Muṇḍaka 2.2.8–9 (numbering varies by recension; verbatim as quoted in Śaṅkara, Brahma-sūtra-bhāṣya)
stages.sanskrit: bhidyate hṛdayagranthiśchidyante sarvasaṃśayāḥ | kṣīyante cāsya karmāṇi tasmin dṛṣṭe parāvare || hiraṇmaye pare kośe virajaṃ brahma niṣkalam | tacchubhraṃ jyotiṣāṃ jyotis tad yad ātmavido viduḥ
stages.rendering: The knot of the heart is cut, all doubts are sundered, and his works dwindle away, when That is seen — the higher-and-lower. || In the supreme golden sheath is Brahman, stainless, without parts; that is the radiant, the light of lights, which the knowers of the self know.
stages.note: Two motifs appear together here. The 'knot of the heart' is cut and 'his works (karman) dwindle' (kṣīyante) — a dwindling/cessation language alongside the *kṣaya* of fuel that *nirvāṇa* names. And the goal is 'the light of lights' (jyotiṣāṃ jyotiḥ) lodged in the heart's golden sheath: the going-out of bondage on one side, the reaching of the inner light on the other. (Our verbatim is taken from the embedded śruti-quotations in our on-disk Śaṅkara Brahma-sūtra-bhāṣya, which reads 'niṣphalam' for the more common 'niṣkalam'; we print the standard 'niṣkalam' and flag the variant.)

stages.sanskrit: eṣā brāhmī sthitiḥ pārtha naināṃ prāpya vimuhyati | sthitvāsyām anta-kāle 'pi brahma-nirvāṇam ṛcchati
stages.rendering: This is the Brahman-founded standing, Pārtha; having reached it one is not deluded. Established in it even at the last hour, one comes to the going-out into Brahman (brahma-nirvāṇa).
stages.note: The compound's first occurrence. *Nirvāṇa* — the flame gone out — is bound to *brahman* so that the going-out is at once an attaining: the desire-and-ego fire goes out, the Brahman-state is reached. The text does not invoke the Vedic heart-flame here.

stages.sanskrit: yo 'ntaḥ-sukho 'ntarārāmas tathāntar-jyotir eva yaḥ | sa yogī brahma-nirvāṇaṃ brahma-bhūto 'dhigacchati
stages.rendering: He whose happiness is within, whose delight is within, and whose light too is within (antar-jyotiḥ) — that yogin, having become Brahman, comes to the going-out into Brahman.
stages.note: Here the *Gītā* comes closest to the inner-light strand: the one who reaches *brahma-nirvāṇa* is 'antar-jyotiḥ', having the light within — the same interior luminosity the Upaniṣads localise in the heart. In this verse the figure is light/jyotis rather than the flame of *nir-√vā* as such.

stages.sanskrit: yuñjann evaṃ sadātmānaṃ yogī niyata-mānasaḥ | śāntiṃ nirvāṇa-paramāṃ mat-saṃsthām adhigacchati
stages.rendering: Thus ever yoking the self, the yogin of governed mind attains the peace that culminates in nirvāṇa (nirvāṇa-paramā śānti), the peace that abides in Me.
stages.note: Here *nirvāṇa* qualifies *śānti* (peace) and is referred to Kṛṣṇa ('that abides in Me', mat-saṃsthā) — the term is here referred to a personal absolute, in line with the Viśiṣṭādvaita reading. Cf. the related verse 6.19, where the steadied yogin's mind is 'a lamp in a windless place' (dīpo nivātastho), the one place the *Gītā* makes the flame-figure explicit — and there it is the lamp that does not flicker, rather than one blown out.

translator_note: Do not collapse *brahma-nirvāṇa* into Buddhist nirvāṇa. The *Gītā* uses the word *nirvāṇa* — a flame going out — and joins it to *brahman*: what goes out is the ego-and-desire fire, what is reached is the absolute, Brahman. Render with the going-out image rather than 'extinction', which in English connotes destruction: 'the going-out into Brahman', 'blown out into Brahman', or 'the peace/bliss of Brahman' keep both the flame-image and the positive absolute in view. The Advaitic 'mergence' should not be read as annihilation. Keeping the going-out image marks the difference from the Buddhist usage, which describes the going-out of the fires of greed, hatred, and delusion without affirming a self or Brahman to be liberated.
